elements.sent-2411-001: The Public Health Advocacy Dataset (PHAD) is a comprehensive collection of 5,730 videos related to tobacco products sourced from social media platforms like TikTok and YouTube.
elements.sent-2411-002: This dataset encompasses 4.3 million frames and includes detailed metadata such as user engagement metrics, video descriptions, and search keywords.
elements.sent-2411-003: This is the first dataset with these features providing a valuable resource for analyzing tobacco-related content and its impact.
elements.sent-2411-004: Our research employs a two-stage classification approach, incorporating a Vision-Language (VL) Encoder, demonstrating superior performance in accurately categorizing various types of tobacco products and usage scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-005: The analysis reveals significant user engagement trends, particularly with vaping and e-cigarette content, highlighting areas for targeted public health interventions.
elements.sent-2411-006: The PHAD addresses the need for multi-modal data in public health research, offering insights that can inform regulatory policies and public health strategies.
elements.sent-2411-007: This dataset is a crucial step towards understanding and mitigating the impact of tobacco usage, ensuring that public health efforts are more inclusive and effective.
elements.sent-2411-008: In the rapidly evolving landscape of digital content, short-form videos on platforms like TikTok and YouTube have revolutionized user-generated content, reshaping how information is consumed and shared.
elements.sent-2411-009: This shift has created an urgent need for advanced deep learning applications capable of comprehending the nuances of video content, particularly in addressing emerging public health concerns.
elements.sent-2411-010: While Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown remarkable capabilities in processing textual data, their adaptation to video understanding remains limited.
elements.sent-2411-011: Current state-of-the-art LLM architectures~\cite{liu2024visual, zhu2023minigpt, li2022blip, li2023blip, dai2024instructblip excel in natural language processing but lack explicit support for video inputs.}
elements.sent-2411-012: This limitation is particularly pronounced on platforms where video content is the primary mode of communication and expression.
elements.sent-2411-013: The vast and diverse user bases of platforms like YouTube and TikTok have transformed them into microcosms of content ranging from entertainment to social commentary.
elements.sent-2411-014: However, this digital ecosystem has inadvertently facilitated the promotion of tobacco-related products, raising significant public health concerns~\cite{lee2023cigarette, kwon2020perceptions, jancey2023promotion, sun2023vaping .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Existing LLMs, designed primarily for text-based operations, are ill-equipped to address this issue effectively, as they neglect the rich visual information embedded in video content.
elements.sent-2411-016: Our research addresses this critical gap by proposing an innovative approach at the intersection of deep learning and video analysis.
elements.sent-2411-017: Focusing on the nuanced domain of tobacco-related content, we present a two-stage end-to-end system that extracts essential cues from videos and trains a vision-language model to comprehend and analyze the contextual intricacies within video content.
elements.sent-2411-018: By combining the power of deep learning with the unique challenges presented by video-centric formats, our approach aims to provide a comprehensive solution to the complex issue of tobacco promotion in this dynamic digital space.
elements.sent-2411-019: Our key contributions are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-020: Public Health Advocacy Dataset (PHAD): We introduce a novel, comprehensive dataset of tobacco-related videos from YouTube and TikTok.
elements.sent-2411-021: This dataset includes rich metadata such as user engagement metrics, video descriptions, and search keywords, providing a robust foundation for research in public health and content moderation.
elements.sent-2411-022: Balanced Sampling Strategy: We employ an innovative approach to dataset construction, using TikTok videos as negative samples (containing tobacco product usage or promotion) and YouTube videos as positive samples (without tobacco product usage or promotion).
elements.sent-2411-023: This balanced strategy enhances the study of tobacco usage, addiction, and promotion across different social media platforms.
elements.sent-2411-024: Two-Stage Classification Framework: We propose a novel classification method that integrates a Vision-Language (VL) Encoder.
elements.sent-2411-025: This two-stage approach significantly improves the accuracy of classifying tobacco-related content by leveraging visual and textual features, addressing the limitations of current text-centric language models in video analysis.
elements.sent-2411-026: User Engagement Analysis: Our research uncovers significant trends in user interaction with tobacco-related content, particularly highlighting the high engagement with vaping and e-cigarette videos.
elements.sent-2411-027: These insights provide crucial information for targeted public health interventions and content moderation strategies.
elements.sent-2411-028: Contextual Feature Evaluation: We demonstrate the critical role of contextual features, such as metadata, in enhancing the performance of deep learning models for video content understanding and classification.
elements.sent-2411-029: This contribution underscores the importance of a holistic video analysis approach beyond visual content alone.
elements.sent-2411-030: Through these contributions, our work addresses the pressing need for advanced deep learning applications in video content analysis, particularly in the context of public health concerns related to tobacco promotion on social media platforms.
elements.sent-2411-031: By combining novel dataset creation, innovative modeling techniques, and comprehensive analysis, we provide a foundation for future research and practical applications in content moderation and public health advocacy.
elements.sent-2411-032: This paper unfolds our proposed methodology and expected results, shedding light on the imperative role that advanced deep learning applications can play in addressing emerging challenges in video content moderation and public health initiatives.

elements.sent-2411-034: Recent advancements in video analysis~\cite{wang2021actionclip, chappa2023sogar, chappa2023spartan, tang2018vctree, zhong2024learning, zareian2020bridging, zareian2020learning, lu2016visual, zhong2021learning, ye2021linguistic, nguyen2024type, chappa2024flaash, truong2022otadapt, jalata2022eqadap, chappa2020squeeze, chappa2024advanced, chappa2024public have predominantly employed traditional deep learning algorithms, but the absence of language comprehension limits their efficacy.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Recognizing this gap, there is a shift towards integrating text modalities~\cite{chappa2024react, chappa2024hatt to enhance understanding capabilities.}
elements.sent-2411-036: It underscores the need to evolve existing algorithms, advocating for joint training with Large Language Models (LLMs)~\cite{liu2024visual to achieve superior performance and nuanced comprehension.}
elements.sent-2411-037: Our work builds upon this paradigm shift, proposing a novel approach seamlessly integrating video and language understanding.
elements.sent-2411-038: Through leveraging the power of LLMs, our framework bridges the gap between visual and textual cues, advancing the state-of-the-art in video analysis.
elements.sent-2411-039: This convergence enables a holistic interpretation of video content, opening new avenues for more accurate, context-aware applications.
elements.sent-2411-040: Recently~\cite{kong2023understanding, murthy2023influence, chappa2024advanced used machine learning techniques to understand, specifically on YouTube, how the user profile attributes influence e-cigarette searches and analysis of their content and promotion strategies.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Prior studies~\cite{ntad224 on e-cigarette content in social media have relied on qualitative and text-based machine learning methods.}
elements.sent-2411-042: They developed an image-based computer vision model using a unique dataset of 6,999 Instagram images labeled for eight object classes related to e-cigarettes, demonstrating an innovative approach to automatically and scalably monitor tobacco promotions online.
elements.sent-2411-043: Murthy et al.~\cite{ntad184 have relied on text-based analyses to detect e-cigarette content on social media, which addressed the visual nature of platforms like TikTok by developing a computer vision model that uses YOLOv7~\cite{wang2023yolov7 .}}
elements.sent-2411-044: This model was trained and tested on a dataset of 826 images, annotated for vaping devices, hands, and vapor clouds, extracted from 254 TikTok posts identified via relevant hashtags, showcasing a practical approach for monitoring e-cigarette content on visual social media platforms.
elements.sent-2411-045: The dataset annotation pipeline and data format are detailed in~\cref{annotation_pipeline and~\cref{data_format .}}

elements.sent-2411-048: This dataset comprises a collection of 5,730 videos, with approximately 4,014 sourced from YouTube~\cite{yt and 1,716 from TikTok~\cite{tiktok .}}
elements.sent-2411-049: These platforms were chosen due to their popularity and the vast amount of user-generated content related to tobacco products.
elements.sent-2411-050: The videos in the dataset represent a wide array of content types, including promotional material, personal vlogs, educational content, and public health advertisements.
elements.sent-2411-051: The videos are annotated with several features aimed at facilitating a multifaceted analysis of tobacco use:.
elements.sent-2411-052: User Engagement Metrics: Contains the likes, shares, comments, and views of the videos in the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-053: Type of Tobacco Products: Classifies the tobacco product shown or discussed in the video (e.g., cigarettes, vaping devices, chewing tobacco).
elements.sent-2411-054: Search Keywords: The keywords used to locate the video may indicate common search behaviors and public interest in specific tobacco products.
elements.sent-2411-055: Video Descriptions: Includes a summary of the video content, providing context and additional details not readily apparent from video analysis alone.
elements.sent-2411-056: The dataset totals over 12.3 GB (videos only) in size and is stored in widely used formats suitable for video processing.
elements.sent-2411-057: The metadata for each video includes a unique identifier, the original video URL, and tagged features, as outlined above.
elements.sent-2411-058: The videos collected fall under various licenses, with many available under standard content licenses provided by YouTube~\cite{yt and TikTok~\cite{tiktok .}}
elements.sent-2411-059: For this dataset, we focus on videos that allow for academic and non-commercial use to ensure compliance with licensing agreements.
elements.sent-2411-060: The use of these videos for educational and research purposes aligns with the terms of service of the hosting platforms, which typically permit such use.

This license allows users to share and adapt the dataset, provided that they give appropriate credit, do not use the material for commercial purposes, and distribute their contributions under the same license.
elements.sent-2411-062: The CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license is well-suited for our dataset, as it is intended solely for research and non-commercial purposes.
elements.sent-2411-063: This license includes works licensed under CC-BY (Creative Commons Attribution), CC-BY-SA (Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike), and CC-BY-NC (Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial).

elements.sent-2411-065: By adhering to these licensing terms, we ensure that our machine learning dataset can be used and adapted freely within the research community while respecting the original creators' conditions.
elements.sent-2411-066: The videos were identified using a combination of automated scraping techniques~\cite{api and manual curation to ensure a broad representation of content related to tobacco products.}
elements.sent-2411-067: This approach allowed for the collection of diverse videos, reflecting different contexts and cultural perspectives of tobacco usage and its implications.
elements.sent-2411-068: This dataset was curated to provide a resource for researchers in public health, psychology, and machine learning.
elements.sent-2411-069: It aims to facilitate a deeper understanding of how tobacco products are portrayed on social media and the impact of these portrayals on public perception and behavior.
elements.sent-2411-070: This dataset consists of publicly available videos collected from YouTube and TikTok.
elements.sent-2411-071: Given the public nature of these platforms, individual content creators' consent for each video was not explicitly obtained; however, our data collection and usage strictly adhere to the platforms' terms of service regarding public content.
elements.sent-2411-072: We have implemented rigorous data handling procedures to ensure the dataset is ethically used, particularly for educational and research purposes.
elements.sent-2411-073: It includes:- Compliance with Platform Policies: All data collection has been performed in compliance with the terms of service of the respective platforms~\cite{yt,tiktok , which allow the analysis of publicly shared content for academic and research purposes.}
elements.sent-2411-074: Use Limitation: The dataset is intended solely for non-commercial use, mainly focused on academic research and public health studies.
elements.sent-2411-075: Researchers are encouraged to apply further ethical considerations in their use of the data, especially when publishing results that could impact individuals or communities.
elements.sent-2411-076: Furthermore, the dataset excludes any content that might involve sensitive personal data or content that could compromise the privacy and dignity of individuals without their explicit consent.
elements.sent-2411-077: This careful curation process ensures that the dataset is both a valuable academic resource and a respectful tool that acknowledges the ethical implications of using publicly sourced video content.

elements.sent-2411-101: This section discusses the inherent limitations associated with our dataset due to technical constraints and dataset-specific characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-102: Understanding these limitations is vital for interpreting the findings derived from this dataset accurately and for guiding future enhancements.
elements.sent-2411-103: Video Quality Variability: Despite most videos being in HD resolution, there is significant variability in video quality due to factors like compression, lighting, and camera quality.
elements.sent-2411-104: Such variability can affect the performance of video analysis algorithms, particularly those involving {action recognition and object detection .}
elements.sent-2411-105: Data Incompleteness: Not all videos include comprehensive metadata such as accurate price ranges or complete descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-106: This incompleteness can lead to gaps in analyses, particularly in understanding the full context of tobacco use depicted in the videos.
elements.sent-2411-107: Platform-Specific Biases: The videos sourced from YouTube and TikTok reflect the content guidelines and user demographics of these platforms, which may not be representative of broader or different social media ecosystems.
elements.sent-2411-108: It can limit the generalizability of the findings to other platforms or offline behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-109: Under Representation of Certain Demographics: While the dataset includes videos from a wide range of sources, certain demographic groups, especially those from lower socioeconomic backgrounds or less represented regions, might be underrepresented.
elements.sent-2411-110: It can skew the analysis towards the behaviors and preferences of more active internet users or those from specific geographical areas.
elements.sent-2411-111: Content Creator Bias: Videos created for public viewing are often curated or produced with specific messages in mind, which can introduce bias regarding how tobacco products are portrayed.
elements.sent-2411-112: It might affect the dataset's utility in objectively analyzing tobacco product usage and public perceptions.
elements.sent-2411-113: This section introduces our two-stage pipeline for video content classification, utilizing Resnet-50~\cite{he2016deep as the backbone network for feature extraction.}
elements.sent-2411-114: In the first stage, discriminative features are extracted from input video frames.
elements.sent-2411-115: Video frames \( f_t, f_{t+1 , \ldots, f_{t+N \) are processed independently through the Resnet-50 network to produce feature maps \( x_t, x_{t+1 , \ldots, x_{t+N \), capturing essential spatial characteristics for subsequent classification.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-116: The feature extraction is mathematically represented as: \begin{equation}x_t = F(f_t; \theta)\end{equation} where \( F \) is the feature extractor, parameterized by \( \theta \).
elements.sent-2411-117: The second stage involves classifying the extracted features using various temporal modeling techniques.
elements.sent-2411-118: We explore five different approaches: Vanilla Approach: Features \( x_t \) are directly fed into a simple classifier without temporal modeling. \begin{equation}y_t = C(x_t; \phi)\end{equation} Handcrafted Window: Temporal information is integrated by applying a predefined kernel \( K \) over the features. \begin{equation}\begin{array}{ll} \hat{x}_t = \sum_{i=-m}^{m} K(i) \cdot x_{t+i} y_t = C(\hat{x}_t; \phi) \end{array}\end{equation} 1D Convolutional Layer: A 1D CNN captures temporal dependencies by sliding a convolutional filter over the feature set. \begin{equation}\begin{array}{ll} \hat{x}_t = \sum_{i=-m}^{m} w_i \cdot x_{t+i} y_t = C(\hat{x}_t; \phi) \end{array}\end{equation} Transformer Encoder~\cite{vaswani2017attention: A Transformer Encoder models complex dependencies between time steps using self-attention. \begin{equation}\begin{array}{ll} \hat{x}_t = \text{Attention}(Q(x_t), K(X), V(X)) y_t = C(\hat{x}_t; \phi) \end{array}\end{equation} Her, Q, K, and V are queries, keys, and values of the corresponding features.}
elements.sent-2411-119: Vision-Language Encoder~\cite{jia2021scaling: This encoder provides a contextually enriched classification by combining visual features \( x_t \) with textual descriptors \( z_t \). \begin{equation}\begin{array}{ll} \hat{x}_t = H(x_t, z_t; \psi) y_t = C(\hat{x}_t; \phi) \end{array}\end{equation} Here, \( C \) is the classifier, \( H \) is the fusion function, and \( \phi \) and \( \psi \) are learned parameters during training.}

elements.subsubsection-2411-001: Benchmarking and Comparisons with Existing Datasets
elements.sent-2411-123: To provide a more robust comparison with existing datasets, we conducted an additional experiment comparing PHAD to the dataset from Murthy et al.~\cite{ntad184 .}
elements.sent-2411-124: To facilitate a fair comparison with their three-class dataset, we adapted PHAD by consolidating our eight classes into three broader categories: (1) Vaping devices, (2) Traditional tobacco products, and (3) Non-tobacco content.
elements.sent-2411-125: We then compared the performance of our model on this adapted version of PHAD with the reported results from Murthy et al. \cite{ntad184 .}
elements.sent-2411-126: Table \ref{tab:comparison presents the performance metrics for the Murthy et al.~\cite{ntad184 dataset, our adapted 3-class PHAD, and the full 8-class PHAD.}}

elements.sent-2411-129: As expected, performance decreases with the full 8-class classification, reflecting the increased complexity and granularity of the task.
elements.sent-2411-130: However, this more nuanced classification offers greater utility for detailed tobacco content analysis.

elements.sent-2411-133: The ability to adapt PHAD for different classification granularities while maintaining high performance underscores its value to the research community, offering flexibility for various analytical needs in tobacco-related content studies.

elements.subsubsection-2411-003: Comparison of Different Approaches in Stage-2 of Our Framework
elements.sent-2411-140: In the second stage of our pipeline, we evaluated five approaches to capturing temporal dynamics and context, with the Vision-Language (VL) Encoder outperforming the others.
elements.sent-2411-141: Vanilla Approach: This baseline model, which directly classifies extracted features without temporal modeling, provided moderate performance, serving as a reference point.
elements.sent-2411-142: Handcrafted Window: By applying a predefined kernel to integrate temporal information, this approach improved performance over the vanilla model by emphasizing significant frames.
elements.sent-2411-143: Transformer Encoder: Leveraging self-attention mechanisms, the Transformer Encoder excelled at modeling complex dependencies across time steps, showing significant improvement in classification accuracy, especially for longer sequences.
elements.sent-2411-144: Vision-Language Encoder: Combining visual features with textual descriptors, the VL Encoder achieved the best performance.
elements.sent-2411-145: This approach provided a comprehensive understanding of video content by processing annotated text labels alongside visual cues, resulting in the most contextually enriched classification outputs.
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{stage_two_comparison_bold_max.png} \caption{Comparison of different approaches in the second stage of our framework. Best viewed in color and zoom.} \label{fig:stage_two_comparison}
elements.sent-2411-146: Significance of 2-Stage Approach: To understand the impact of the two-stage process, we conducted an ablation study comparing the single-stage and two-stage approaches.

elements.sent-2411-151: Our analysis of the PHAD reveals significant insights into tobacco-related content on social media.
elements.sent-2411-152: This section discusses our results, dataset limitations, and future research directions.
elements.sent-2411-153: Implications for Public Health and Policy: Insights from PHAD have several implications for public health and policy:- Targeted Interventions: Focus on vaping and e-cigarette content for educational campaigns and regulatory measures to curb use among younger populations.
elements.sent-2411-154: Enhanced Monitoring and Regulation: Accurate classification aids regulators in monitoring compliance with advertising laws and identifying illegal tobacco sales or underage use.
elements.sent-2411-155: Informing Policy Decisions: Policymakers can use these insights to inform tobacco control strategies and public health campaigns.
elements.sent-2411-156: Future Work: Future research should address limitations and explore additional avenues:- Expanding the Dataset: Include data from more social media platforms and regions for a comprehensive view.
elements.sent-2411-157: Improving Data Quality: Enhance metadata quality and completeness through sophisticated data collection and annotation methods.
elements.sent-2411-158: Advanced Analysis Techniques: Explore advanced machine learning techniques, such as reinforcement learning and generative models, to improve content classification.
elements.sent-2411-159: Longitudinal Studies: Conduct studies to understand trends in tobacco use and public perception over time.
elements.sent-2411-160: We introduced the Public Health Advocacy Dataset, a comprehensive dataset containing videos related to tobacco products sourced from YouTube and TikTok.
elements.sent-2411-161: This dataset included detailed metadata such as user engagement metrics, video descriptions, and search keywords, providing a rich resource for analyzing tobacco-related content and its impact.
elements.sent-2411-162: Our analysis demonstrated that the PHAD significantly improved the performance of classification tasks, especially when using a two-stage method incorporating a Vision-Language (VL) Encoder.
elements.sent-2411-163: This approach effectively leveraged visual and textual features, enhancing the model's ability to categorize various tobacco products and usage scenarios accurately.
elements.sent-2411-164: This dataset provided valuable insights into public engagement trends, such as the high interaction with vaping and e-cigarette content, which is crucial for informing public health interventions and regulatory policies.
elements.sent-2411-165: The PHAD contributed to a more nuanced understanding of tobacco use and its portrayal on social media by addressing the need for multi-modal data in public health research.
elements.sent-2411-166: To that end, the PHAD has been a significant step forward in providing researchers and policymakers with the tools needed to better understand and address the challenges posed by tobacco use.
elements.sent-2411-167: The dataset's diverse and comprehensive nature helped combat biases and ensure public health strategies are more inclusive and effective.

elements.sent-2411-178: For what purpose was the dataset created? Was there a specific task in mind? Was there a specific gap that needed to be filled? Please provide a description.
elements.sent-2411-179: The PHAD was created to address the growing need for comprehensive data on tobacco-related content across social media platforms, specifically YouTube and TikTok.
elements.sent-2411-180: The dataset was developed with the following objectives in mind: 1.
elements.sent-2411-181: Provide a Rich Resource for Analyzing Tobacco-Related Content: PHAD includes diverse videos related to various tobacco products, accompanied by detailed metadata such as user engagement metrics, video descriptions, and search keywords.
elements.sent-2411-182: This comprehensive dataset allows researchers to analyze the nature and impact of tobacco-related content on social media.. 2.
elements.sent-2411-183: Enhance Public Health Research: By offering a multi-modal dataset that includes both visual and textual features, PHAD aims to improve the accuracy and effectiveness of models used in public health research.
elements.sent-2411-184: The dataset helps in understanding user engagement trends and the influence of tobacco-related content, which is crucial for developing targeted public health interventions.. 3.
elements.sent-2411-185: Fill the Gap in Existing Datasets: Existing datasets often lack the depth and breadth required for nuanced analysis of tobacco-related content.
elements.sent-2411-186: PHAD addresses this gap by providing a large-scale, high-quality dataset that captures a wide range of tobacco products and usage scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-187: It also includes important contextual information that is typically missing in other datasets.. 4.
elements.sent-2411-188: Support Non-Commercial Public Health Efforts: The dataset is licensed under the CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license, ensuring that it can be freely used for research and non-commercial purposes.
elements.sent-2411-189: This licensing choice facilitates the widespread dissemination and utilization of the dataset in academic and public health research, promoting collaborative efforts to combat tobacco use.
elements.sent-2411-190: The creation of PHAD is a significant step towards understanding and mitigating the impact of tobacco usage, providing researchers and policymakers with the tools needed to develop more effective public health strategies.

elements.sent-2411-204: Search Keywords: Metadata also includes search keywords associated with the videos, which offer insights into how users discover and interact with tobacco-related content.. 5.

elements.sent-2411-287: Diversity of Content: The dataset aimed to include a diverse range of tobacco-related content, including educational videos, product reviews, user experiences, and public health messages.

elements.sent-2411-289: Geographic and Demographic Variety: Efforts were made to include videos from different geographic regions and demographic groups to ensure the dataset reflects the global nature of social media usage and the varied cultural contexts of tobacco use.
elements.sent-2411-291: Relevance and Quality Checks: Each video was manually reviewed to ensure it met the relevance criteria and quality standards for inclusion in the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-292: Irrelevant or low-quality videos were excluded to maintain the dataset's integrity.
elements.sent-2411-293: Balancing Categories: The sampling process was adjusted to balance the representation of different tobacco product categories (e.g., cigarettes, e-cigarettes, vaping devices, smokeless tobacco) to avoid over-representation of any single category.
elements.sent-2411-294: By combining deterministic and purposive sampling strategies, the PHAD was curated to provide a rich and diverse dataset that represents the larger set of tobacco-related content on social media and is highly relevant for public health research.

elements.sent-2411-326: Behavioral Analysis: Understanding user behavior and engagement with tobacco-related content can help in designing effective public health campaigns.

elements.sent-2411-328: Content Moderation: Developing algorithms to detect and moderate harmful or misleading tobacco-related content.

elements.sent-2411-331: Sentiment Analysis: Analyzing the sentiment of comments and descriptions related to tobacco products.
elements.sent-2411-332: Topic Modeling: Identifying key themes and topics discussed in relation to tobacco use.
elements.sent-2411-333: Object Detection: Detecting and classifying various tobacco products in video frames.

elements.sent-2411-341: Temporal Relevance: The dataset captures a specific period (January 2024 to June 2024), and user behaviors and trends can change over time.
elements.sent-2411-342: Future users should consider the temporal context when drawing conclusions and ensure that the findings are relevant to the current landscape.
elements.sent-2411-343: Ethical Use: The dataset includes sensitive content related to tobacco use, which can have significant public health implications.
elements.sent-2411-344: Future users should ensure that their analyses and applications do not perpetuate stereotypes or lead to unfair treatment of individuals or groups.
elements.sent-2411-345: It is crucial to maintain ethical standards and respect privacy and consent when using the dataset.

elements.sent-2411-348: Regular Updates: To maintain temporal relevance, users should consider updating the dataset with more recent data periodically.
elements.sent-2411-349: Ethical Guidelines: Adhere to ethical guidelines and best practices in data usage, including obtaining necessary permissions, respecting privacy, and avoiding the dissemination of harmful or misleading information.
elements.sent-2411-350: By considering these factors, future users can leverage the PHAD effectively while minimizing the risk of undesirable harm and ensuring the responsible use of the dataset.

elements.sent-2411-393: We used four NVIDIA A6000-powered GPUs to perform the training, validation, and testing of our dataset.
elements.sent-2411-394: The PHAD dataset is sourced from public-domain videos on YouTube and TikTok.
elements.sent-2411-395: The initial step involves scraping videos using specific search keywords related to tobacco products.
elements.sent-2411-396: These videos are then downloaded and organized into train and validation sets based on predefined ratios.
elements.sent-2411-397: Before annotation, the videos undergo several preprocessing steps:.
elements.sent-2411-398: Frame Extraction: Videos are processed to extract frames at a rate of 30 frames per second (fps) to ensure high temporal resolution for subsequent analysis.
elements.sent-2411-399: Resolution Standardization: All frames are standardized to a uniform resolution to maintain consistency across the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-400: Metadata Extraction: Metadata such as video descriptions, user engagement metrics (views, likes, comments, shares), and search keywords are extracted and linked to the corresponding videos and frames.
elements.sent-2411-401: The annotation process involves labeling each frame with relevant information:.
elements.sent-2411-402: Tobacco Product Type: Each frame is annotated with the type of tobacco product visible, such as cigarettes, e-cigarettes, vaping devices, or smokeless tobacco.
elements.sent-2411-403: User Engagement Metrics: Metadata related to user interactions (e.g., views, likes, comments, shares) are linked to the frames.
elements.sent-2411-404: Textual Descriptions: Frames are annotated with textual descriptions derived from video captions and comments to provide context.
elements.sent-2411-405: Temporal Segmentation: Frames are temporally segmented to highlight key moments within the videos, such as when a tobacco product is first shown or discussed.
elements.sent-2411-406: Post-processing ensures the quality and consistency of annotations:.
elements.sent-2411-407: Validation: Automated scripts validate the consistency of annotations, checking for missing or incorrect labels.
elements.sent-2411-408: Normalization: Annotations are normalized to ensure uniformity in labeling conventions across the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-409: Despite automated processes, human oversight is essential to maintain the dataset's quality:.
elements.sent-2411-410: Manual Review: Human annotators review a subset of the annotated frames to verify accuracy and correct any discrepancies.
elements.sent-2411-411: Curation: Annotators curate frames to ensure they are representative of the entire video, selecting keyframes that highlight significant moments.
elements.sent-2411-412: Filtering: Irrelevant frames or those with poor visual quality are filtered out.
elements.sent-2411-413: Additionally, any content violating platform guidelines or depicting illegal activities is removed.
elements.sent-2411-414: The final step in the pipeline involves rigorous quality assurance:.
elements.sent-2411-415: Inter-Annotator Agreement: The consistency of annotations is measured using inter-annotator agreement metrics to ensure reliability.
elements.sent-2411-416: Random Sampling: Random samples of annotated frames are periodically reviewed to maintain high standards of annotation quality.
elements.sent-2411-417: Feedback Loop: Annotators provide feedback on the annotation process, which is used to refine guidelines and improve future annotation efforts.
elements.sent-2411-418: The PHAD dataset adheres to strict ethical guidelines:.
elements.sent-2411-419: Licensing: The dataset is licensed under the CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license, allowing for non-commercial use with appropriate attribution.
elements.sent-2411-420: Privacy: All videos included in the dataset are publicly available, and no personally identifiable information is included.
elements.sent-2411-421: Ethics Review: The dataset creation process underwent an ethics review to ensure compliance with relevant guidelines and standards.

elements.subsection-2411-023: Python Script to Download and Organize the Dataset
elements.sent-2411-424: Use the following script to download all the videos into their corresponding 'train' and 'val' directories.
elements.sent-2411-425: The json files are available to download at \url{https://anonymo-user.github.io/PHAD/ .}
elements.lstlisting-2411-002: [language=iPython] import os import json import requests import glob # Define paths to the directory containing JSON files and the output directory json_dir = '/path/to/json_files' output_dir = '/path/to/output' # Define output directories for train and val train_dir = os.path.join(output_dir, 'train') val_dir = os.path.join(output_dir, 'val') # Create directories if they do not exist os.makedirs(train_dir, exist_ok=True) os.makedirs(val_dir, exist_ok=True) def download_videos(json_path, output_folder): with open(json_path, 'r') as f: data = json.load(f) for item in data: if 'mediaUrls/0' in item: video_url = item['mediaUrls/0'] video_id = video_url.split('/')[-1] video_path = os.path.join(output_folder, f"{video_id}.mp4") if not os.path.exists(video_path): try: r = requests.get(video_url) r.raise_for_status() with open(video_path, 'wb') as f: f.write(r.content) print(f"Downloaded {video_path}") except requests.RequestException as e: print(f"Failed to download {video_url}: {e}") # Process all JSON files in the directory for json_file in glob.glob(os.path.join(json_dir, '*.json')): file_type = os.path.basename(json_file).split('_')[-1].split('.')[0] if 'train' in file_type: download_videos(json_file, train_dir) elif 'val' in file_type: download_videos(json_file, val_dir)
elements.sent-2411-426: Once the videos are downloaded to the corresponding 'train' and 'val' directories, then extract the frames of all the videos at 30 frames per sec using the following python script.
elements.sent-2411-427: Our experiments were conducted on a system equipped with the following specifications:.
elements.sent-2411-428: GPU: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090 with 24GB VRAM.
elements.sent-2411-433: The dataset comprises videos from YouTube and TikTok related to tobacco usage.
elements.sent-2411-434: The videos were preprocessed to extract frames and corresponding textual descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-436: Frames were extracted at a rate of 30 frames per second using OpenCV.
elements.sent-2411-437: Each video was resized to a resolution of 224x224 pixels to ensure uniformity.
elements.sent-2411-438: Textual descriptions were obtained from video metadata and manually annotated descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-439: Text preprocessing included tokenization, stop-word removal, and stemming.
elements.sent-2411-440: The dataset was split into three subsets:.
elements.sent-2411-444: For the feature extraction stage, we used a convolutional neural network (CNN) as the backbone.
elements.sent-2411-445: Specifically, we employed ResNet-50 pre-trained on ImageNet due to its balance between performance and computational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-446: The feature extraction process is described as follows: \begin{equation}x_t = F(f_t; \theta)\end{equation} where \(x_t\) represents the feature vector extracted from the video frame \(f_t\), and \(\theta\) denotes the parameters of the ResNet-50 network.
elements.sent-2411-447: We experimented with various models for the second stage of our framework, including a vanilla classifier, handcrafted window approach, 1D CNN, Transformer Encoder, and Vision-Language Encoder.
elements.sent-2411-449: A simple fully connected neural network was used for classification.
elements.sent-2411-451: A predefined kernel was applied over a sliding window of features.
elements.sent-2411-453: A 1D CNN with 3 convolutional layers followed by max-pooling and fully connected layers.
elements.sent-2411-455: A Transformer Encoder with 4 layers, 8 attention heads, and hidden size of 256.
elements.sent-2411-457: Combines visual features with textual features using a multimodal fusion technique.
elements.sent-2411-458: Visual features were extracted using ResNet-50.
elements.sent-2411-459: Textual features were extracted using a pre-trained BERT model.
elements.sent-2411-460: Fusion: Concatenation followed by a fully connected layer.
elements.sent-2411-461: Hyperparameters for the models were tuned using the validation set.
elements.sent-2411-462: The final values used for training are listed below:.
elements.sent-2411-464: Learning Rate: Adjusted for each model as specified above.
elements.sent-2411-465: Loss Function: Binary Cross-Entropy Loss for multi-label classification.
elements.sent-2411-466: All models were initialized with pre-trained weights where applicable (e.g., ResNet-50, BERT).
elements.sent-2411-467: Adam optimizer was used for most models, with AdamW specifically for the Transformer Encoder due to its better handling of weight decay.
elements.sent-2411-468: A learning rate scheduler with a decay factor of 0.1 was applied every 10 epochs if the validation loss did not improve.
elements.sent-2411-469: Dropout with a rate of 0.5 was applied to prevent overfitting.
elements.sent-2411-470: Models were evaluated on the validation set after each epoch.
elements.sent-2411-471: The best model was selected based on the highest F1-score.
elements.sent-2411-472: In a multi-label classification problem, each instance (video) belongs to multiple classes simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-473: Therefore, the evaluation metrics need to account for the presence of multiple labels.
elements.sent-2411-474: Here we present the used metrics for multi-label classification: 1.
elements.sent-2411-475: Hamming Loss Hamming Loss is the fraction of labels that are incorrectly predicted.
elements.sent-2411-476: It is calculated as: \begin{equation}Hamming Loss = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{L} \sum_{j=1}^{L} 1(y_{ij} \neq \hat{y}_{ij})\end{equation} where \(N\) is the number of instances, \(L\) is the number of labels, \(y_{ij}\) is the true label, \(\hat{y _{ij}\) is the predicted label, and \(\mathbf{1}\) is the indicator function.. 2.}
elements.sent-2411-477: Accuracy Multi-label accuracy (also known as subset accuracy) measures the proportion of instances where the entire set of predicted labels exactly matches the set of true labels: \begin{equation}Accuracy = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} 1(Y_i = \hat{Y}_i)\end{equation} where \(Y_i\) and \(\hat{Y _i\) are the true and predicted label sets for instance \(i\).. 3.}
elements.sent-2411-478: Precision, Recall, and F1-score For multi-label classification, precision, recall, and F1-score can be computed in two ways: macro-averaged and micro-averaged.

elements.sent-2411-481: To further validate our approach, we conducted two additional experiments: (1) a comparison with advanced models like GPT-4V, and (2) an evaluation of our proposed model architecture against other vision-language models.
elements.sent-2411-482: We compared the performance of GPT-4V~\cite{openai2024gpt4technicalreport with our proposed VL Encoder method for tobacco usage and promotion detection on the PHAD dataset.}

elements.sent-2411-485: While this experiment provides valuable insights, we emphasize that PHAD complements rather than competes with advanced general-purpose models.

elements.sent-2411-488: Quantifiable metrics: Allows for precise evaluation using standard metrics.
elements.sent-2411-489: Reproducibility and consistency: Enables fair comparisons between different approaches over time.
elements.sent-2411-490: Domain-specific training data: Beneficial for fine-tuning models for optimal performance in public health monitoring.
elements.sent-2411-491: Interpretability and bias mitigation: Allows for analysis across different types of tobacco products and usage scenarios.

elements.sent-2411-495: These results highlight several key advantages of our proposed architecture:.
elements.sent-2411-496: Superior Performance: Our VL Encoder achieves the highest accuracy, precision, and recall.
elements.sent-2411-497: Two-Stage Advantage: The performance gap between our full VL Encoder and its single-stage variant underscores the importance of our two-stage approach.
elements.sent-2411-498: Multimodal Capability: Our architecture effectively leverages visual and textual information.
elements.sent-2411-499: Specialization Benefits: The results show how a tailored architecture can outperform more general approaches in specialized tasks like tobacco usage detection.
elements.sent-2411-500: While our architecture builds upon established vision-language models, its specific design choices are optimized for the challenges of tobacco content detection.
elements.sent-2411-501: The two-stage approach allows for more nuanced feature extraction and classification, particularly useful for distinguishing between tobacco products and usage scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-502: These additional experiments demonstrate the value of our proposed approach in the specific domain of tobacco usage detection, showing how tailored approaches can significantly enhance performance in specialized public health monitoring tasks.
elements.sent-2411-503: The PHAD offers a wide range of applications across different fields, primarily focusing on public health, social media analysis, and machine learning research.
elements.sent-2411-504: Below are some key use cases that demonstrate the potential of this dataset.
elements.sent-2411-505: Understanding Tobacco Consumption Trends: Researchers can analyze the dataset to identify patterns and trends in tobacco consumption, such as the popularity of different tobacco products over time and across various demographics.
elements.sent-2411-506: It can inform public health campaigns and policy-making.. 2.
elements.sent-2411-507: Evaluating the Impact of Public Health Campaigns: By monitoring user engagement metrics and sentiment analysis on videos related to public health campaigns, researchers can assess the effectiveness of these campaigns in changing public perceptions and behaviors related to tobacco use.. 3.
elements.sent-2411-508: Identifying At-Risk Populations: The dataset can help identify specific demographics that are more likely to engage with tobacco-related content.
elements.sent-2411-509: This information can be used to tailor public health interventions to target these at-risk populations more effectively.
elements.sent-2411-510: Sentiment Analysis: Using the metadata and comments associated with the videos, researchers can perform sentiment analysis to understand public opinion about various tobacco products and regulations.
elements.sent-2411-511: This can help in gauging the public's response to new policies or health warnings.. 2.
elements.sent-2411-512: Virality and Information Spread: The dataset can be used to study how tobacco-related content spreads on social media platforms.
elements.sent-2411-513: Researchers can analyze factors that contribute to the virality of such content, including the role of influencers and the impact of social networks.. 3.
elements.sent-2411-514: Content Moderation and Policy Compliance: Social media platforms and policymakers can use the dataset to develop and refine content moderation algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-515: This can help in identifying and mitigating the spread of content that violates platform policies or promotes illegal tobacco sales.
elements.sent-2411-516: Developing Classification Models: The dataset provides a rich source of labeled data for training machine learning models to classify different types of tobacco-related content.
elements.sent-2411-517: This can include distinguishing between various tobacco products or identifying promotional versus informational content.. 2.
elements.sent-2411-518: Enhancing Natural Language Processing (NLP) Techniques: The textual metadata and comments in the dataset can be used to train and evaluate NLP models.
elements.sent-2411-519: Researchers can develop more sophisticated sentiment analysis, topic modeling, and keyword extraction techniques tailored to public health data.. 3.
elements.sent-2411-520: Multimodal Learning: The combination of video, audio, and text data in the dataset offers an opportunity for advancing multimodal learning algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-521: Researchers can explore how integrating different data types can improve the performance of models in tasks such as video classification and behavior analysis.
elements.sent-2411-522: Monitoring Compliance with Advertising Regulations: Regulators can use the dataset to monitor compliance with advertising regulations related to tobacco products.
elements.sent-2411-523: This can help identify content that may be in violation of laws and take appropriate enforcement actions.. 2.
elements.sent-2411-524: Informing Policy Decisions: The insights gained from analyzing the dataset can inform policy decisions regarding tobacco control.
elements.sent-2411-525: For example, understanding which products are most popular among youth can lead to targeted restrictions and educational campaigns.. 3.
elements.sent-2411-526: Assessing the Effectiveness of Policy Changes: By analyzing engagement metrics before and after policy changes, researchers can assess the effectiveness of these changes in reducing the prevalence of tobacco-related content and its impact on public health.. \end{appendices.}
